_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows the curriculum in Runestone Foundations of Python Programming, Think Python, and OpenStax Introduction to Python Programming. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the sources guide topic selection only.
title: File Handling
summary: Read and write text files without leaving resources open.
refresher: Open files with a `with` statement so Python closes them automatically. Use mode `"r"` to read, `"w"` to replace a file, and `"a"` to add at the end. Text read from a file is a string and often includes newline characters.
refresher_code:
with open("notes.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as file:
    text = file.read()
print(text)

blocks.body: Variables disappear when a program stops, but files remain. That makes files useful for saved settings, reports, and data that another program will read later. File work also introduces failures such as a missing path or denied access.

blocks.caption: The `with` block owns the open file. When the indented block ends, Python closes it even if an error interrupted the work.
blocks.code:
with open("courses.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as file:
    file.write("CS101\n")
    file.write("MATH120\n")

with open("courses.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as file:
    print(file.read())

blocks.body: Mode `w` starts a fresh file and erases old contents. Mode `a` writes after what is already there. Mode `r` reads and raises FileNotFoundError if the path does not exist.

blocks.caption: Keeping the read inside `with` avoids trying to use a closed file later. The text itself can be saved and used after the block.
blocks.wrong:
with open("notes.txt") as file:
    pass
text = file.read()
blocks.right:
with open("notes.txt") as file:
    text = file.read()
print(text)

blocks.title: Writing can replace real data
blocks.body: Check the mode before running file code. Opening an existing file with `"w"` clears it immediately. Choose `"a"` when the goal is to preserve the old text and add another record.

blocks.prompt: Which mode adds new text without deleting existing text?
blocks.why: Append mode `a` positions writes at the end. Write mode `w` replaces existing contents, while read mode does not permit writing.

blocks.body: Reading line by line avoids loading a large file all at once. Each line usually ends with `\n`; `strip()` removes surrounding whitespace when that newline is not part of the data.

choices: To remove the newline around each line
To convert each line to a number
blocks.why: File iteration keeps the newline character at the end of most lines. `strip()` removes it so `print` does not add an extra blank line.

blocks.title: File handling checklist
items: Specify UTF-8 for text you control.
Confirm whether the mode reads, replaces, or appends.
Expect missing paths and permission errors.
Process large files one line at a time.
